---
title: "Employee Social Media Monitoring"
description: "Primary research study on employee perceptions of workplace social media monitoring. Survey data from 64 respondents analysed using Python."
date: 2026-04-14
image: ../../images/dissertation.jpg
categories:
  - "Python"
  - "Data Analysis"
  - "Primary Research"
---

## Overview

This dissertation examined a question at the intersection of workplace rights and
digital privacy: **how do organisations monitor employees' personal social media
activity, and how do employees perceive the fairness and legitimacy of these
practices?**

Primary research was conducted via an original survey distributed across Ireland,
Canada, and internationally. After filtering for employment eligibility, **64 valid
responses** were collected and analysed using Python.

::: {.callout-note}
## Research Question
How do organisations monitor and respond to employees' personal social media
activity, and how do employees perceive the fairness and legitimacy of these
organisational practices?
:::

---

## Research Journey

The dissertation was completed across four sequential components:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Research Proposal - Component 1] --> B[Literature Review - Component 2]
    B --> C[Research Methodology - Component 3]
    C --> D[Research Results - Component 4]
    style A fill:#264653,color:#fff
    style B fill:#264653,color:#fff
    style C fill:#264653,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2a9d8f,color:#fff
```

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **1 - Research Proposal** | Defined the research question, objectives, and justification for primary research |
| **2 - Literature Review** | Reviewed existing academic literature on workplace monitoring, privacy, and legitimacy |
| **3 - Research Methodology** | Designed the survey instrument, ethical framework, and data collection approach |
| **4 - Research Results** | Collected and analysed 64 survey responses using Python |

[View Research Results PDF](../../dissertation/dennis-chumakov-dissertation-results.pdf){target="_blank"}

---

## Methodology

The survey instrument included:

- **Demographic questions** - sector, tenure, management level
- **Policy awareness questions** - whether a policy exists and is communicated
- **Likert-scale questions (Q9-Q14)** - perceptions of monitoring, discipline, and behavioural impact
- **Scenario questions (Q12-Q13)** - comparing appropriateness of discipline across two real-world scenarios
- **Open-ended questions (Q16-Q18)** - qualitative themes around fairness, intervention, and improvement

A **Wilcoxon signed-rank test** was used to assess whether the difference in
disciplinary appropriateness between the two scenarios was statistically significant.[^1]

[^1]: The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is a non-parametric test used when data does
not meet the assumptions of normality - appropriate here given the ordinal Likert
scale used.

---

## Analysis and Findings

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show setup and data loading"
#| echo: true

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from scipy.stats import wilcoxon
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

df = pd.read_csv('survey_sample.csv')

# Filter valid respondents
df = df[df.iloc[:, 6] != 'No'].copy()
df = df.reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"Dataset loaded: {len(df)} respondents")
```

### Respondent Demographics

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show demographics code"
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of respondents by sector"

sector_counts = df['sector'].value_counts().reset_index()
sector_counts.columns = ['Sector', 'Count']

fig1 = px.bar(sector_counts, x='Sector', y='Count',
              title='Respondents by Sector',
              color_discrete_sequence=['#264653'])
fig1.update_layout(showlegend=False, plot_bgcolor='white',
                   xaxis=dict(showgrid=False),
                   yaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor='#f0f0f0'))
fig1.show()
```

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show tenure code"
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of respondents by length of employment"

tenure_order = ['Less than 1 year', '1-3 years', '4-7 years']
tenure_counts = df['tenure'].value_counts().reindex(tenure_order).reset_index()
tenure_counts.columns = ['Tenure', 'Count']

fig2 = px.bar(tenure_counts, x='Tenure', y='Count',
              title='Respondents by Length of Employment',
              color_discrete_sequence=['#264653'])
fig2.update_layout(showlegend=False, plot_bgcolor='white',
                   xaxis=dict(showgrid=False),
                   yaxis=dict(showgrid=True, gridcolor='#f0f0f0'))
fig2.show()
```

---

### Perceptions of Monitoring and Privacy

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show perceptions code"
#| fig-cap: "Mean Likert scores for Q9 - perceptions of social media monitoring (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree)"

q9_labels = [
    'Right to monitor public SM',
    'Monitoring = invasion of privacy',
    'Only intervene if affects org',
    'Monitoring improves reputation',
    'Creates culture of mistrust'
]
q9_cols = ['q9a_right_to_monitor_num','q9b_invasion_privacy_num',
           'q9c_intervene_if_affects_org_num','q9d_improves_reputation_num',
           'q9e_culture_mistrust_num']
q9_means = [round(df[col].mean(), 2) for col in q9_cols]

fig3 = go.Figure()
fig3.add_trace(go.Bar(x=q9_labels, y=q9_means,
                      marker_color='#264653',
                      text=q9_means, textposition='outside'))
fig3.add_hline(y=3, line_dash='dash', line_color='#e63946',
               annotation_text='Neutral (3.0)', annotation_position='right')
fig3.update_layout(title='Perceptions of Monitoring and Privacy (Q9)',
                   yaxis=dict(range=[0, 5.5], title='Mean Score (1-5)',
                              gridcolor='#f0f0f0'),
                   xaxis=dict(title='Statement', showgrid=False),
                   plot_bgcolor='white')
fig3.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key Finding
Respondents strongly agreed that monitoring creates a **culture of mistrust**
(mean 3.97) and that monitoring constitutes an **invasion of privacy** (mean 3.97),
while showing moderate agreement that employers should **only intervene when
organisational impact is direct** (mean 4.05).
:::

---

### Scenario Comparison - When is Discipline Appropriate?

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show scenario comparison code"
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of disciplinary appropriateness scores across two scenarios - Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirms statistically significant difference (p < 0.0001)"

stat, p_value = wilcoxon(df['q12_scenario1_num'], df['q13_scenario2_num'])

scenario_labels = ['Scenario 1 - Political Opinion',
                   'Scenario 2 - Offensive/Viral Post']
scenario_means = [round(df['q12_scenario1_num'].mean(), 2),
                  round(df['q13_scenario2_num'].mean(), 2)]

fig5 = go.Figure()
fig5.add_trace(go.Bar(x=scenario_labels, y=scenario_means,
                      marker_color=['#2a9d8f', '#e76f51'],
                      text=scenario_means, textposition='outside'))
fig5.add_hline(y=3, line_dash='dash', line_color='#e63946',
               annotation_text='Neutral (3.0)', annotation_position='right')
fig5.add_annotation(x=0.5, y=5.1, xref='paper',
                    text=f'Wilcoxon W={round(stat,1)}, p<0.0001***',
                    showarrow=False, font=dict(color='darkred', size=11),
                    bgcolor='lightyellow', bordercolor='darkred')
fig5.update_layout(title='Disciplinary Appropriateness by Scenario (Q12 vs Q13)',
                   yaxis=dict(range=[0, 5.5], title='Mean Appropriateness (1-5)',
                              gridcolor='#f0f0f0'),
                   xaxis=dict(showgrid=False),
                   plot_bgcolor='white')
fig5.show()
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Statistical Finding
The Wilcoxon signed-rank test confirmed a **statistically significant difference**
between the two scenarios (p < 0.0001). Respondents rated discipline for an
offensive/viral post (Scenario 2, mean 3.97) as significantly more appropriate
than for a political opinion post (Scenario 1, mean 2.40) - suggesting that
context and content type are central to perceptions of legitimacy.
:::

---

### Behavioural Impact of Monitoring Awareness

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show behavioural impact code"
#| fig-cap: "Mean scores for Q14 - how awareness of monitoring affects employee behaviour online"

q14_labels = [
    'Monitoring affects how I use SM',
    'I avoid posting opinions online',
    'I consider how posts reflect on employer',
    'Monitoring reduces trust in management',
    'I feel comfortable expressing opinions'
]
q14_cols = ['q14a_monitoring_affects_usage_num','q14b_avoid_opinions_num',
            'q14c_consider_employer_reflection_num','q14d_reduces_trust_num',
            'q14e_comfortable_expressing_num']
q14_means = [round(df[col].mean(), 2) for col in q14_cols]

fig6 = go.Figure()
fig6.add_trace(go.Bar(x=q14_labels, y=q14_means,
                      marker_color='#264653',
                      text=q14_means, textposition='outside'))
fig6.add_hline(y=3, line_dash='dash', line_color='#e63946',
               annotation_text='Neutral (3.0)', annotation_position='right')
fig6.update_layout(title='Behavioural Impact of Monitoring Awareness (Q14)',
                   yaxis=dict(range=[0, 5.5], title='Mean Score (1-5)',
                              gridcolor='#f0f0f0'),
                   xaxis=dict(showgrid=False),
                   plot_bgcolor='white')
fig6.show()
```

---

### Descriptive Statistics Summary

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show summary table code"

all_cols = ['q9a_right_to_monitor_num','q9b_invasion_privacy_num',
            'q9c_intervene_if_affects_org_num','q9d_improves_reputation_num',
            'q9e_culture_mistrust_num','q10_appropriate_discipline_num',
            'q11_discipline_proportionate_num','q12_scenario1_num',
            'q13_scenario2_num','q14a_monitoring_affects_usage_num',
            'q14b_avoid_opinions_num','q14c_consider_employer_reflection_num',
            'q14d_reduces_trust_num','q14e_comfortable_expressing_num']

labels = [
    'Q9a: Right to monitor public SM',
    'Q9b: Monitoring = invasion of privacy',
    'Q9c: Only intervene if affects org',
    'Q9d: Monitoring improves reputation',
    'Q9e: Creates culture of mistrust',
    'Q10: Discipline is appropriate',
    'Q11: Discipline is proportionate',
    'Q12: Scenario 1 - Political opinion',
    'Q13: Scenario 2 - Offensive/viral post',
    'Q14a: Monitoring affects usage',
    'Q14b: Avoid posting opinions',
    'Q14c: Consider employer reflection',
    'Q14d: Monitoring reduces trust',
    'Q14e: Comfortable expressing freely'
]

summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Question': labels,
    'Mean': [round(df[c].mean(), 2) for c in all_cols],
    'Std Dev': [round(df[c].std(), 2) for c in all_cols],
    'Min': [df[c].min() for c in all_cols],
    'Max': [df[c].max() for c in all_cols]
})

print(summary.to_string(index=False))
```

---

## Reflections

This project was my first experience conducting original primary research from
design through to statistical analysis. Building the survey instrument, collecting
responses across multiple countries, and then writing Python to clean, encode,
and visualise the data gave me an end-to-end understanding of the research process
that no secondary data project could replicate.

The Wilcoxon test result was the most surprising finding - the scale of the
difference between the two scenarios (mean gap of 1.57) suggested that employees
draw a very clear line between political expression and harmful/offensive content
when judging disciplinary legitimacy.

---

::: {.small-text}
*Cover image via [Freepik](https://freepik.com)*
:::
